does: This paper systematically investigates how to control personality traits in large language models, specifically studying whether LLMs can be steered toward extroverted or introverted behavior. The authors evaluate four intervention methods: continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and prompt engineering. They establish a hierarchy of effectiveness for personality control, finding that prompting achieves the highest immediate influence but produces fragile personality changes, while SFT yields more robust and persistent personality modification. The study reveals that personality control success varies significantly by method, with prompt-based approaches being accessible but unstable compared to training-based approaches that embed personality more durably into model weights.
connects: This work builds on recent research examining emergent personality and behavioral properties in large language models, extending prior work on LLM alignment, controllability, and fine-tuning effectiveness. It responds to concerns about toxic or unwanted emergent behaviors in LLMs by investigating the mechanisms through which personality emerges and can be systematically modified. The paper combines insights from instruction tuning and RLHF literature with personality psychology frameworks, building on established methods like supervised fine-tuning and RLHF that are standard for behavior modification. It also engages with prompt engineering research showing prompts influence model outputs, while asking deeper questions about robustness and persistence of prompt-induced changes versus training-based control.
enables: This work opens avenues for more nuanced personality and behavioral control in deployed LLMs, supporting applications requiring specific personality profiles such as chatbots tailored to user preferences or professional contexts. It points toward developing robust personality specifications as part of model development pipelines, potentially informing best practices for model deployment where personality consistency matters. The finding that prompt-induced personalities lack robustness suggests future work on adversarial testing of personality stability and development of verification methods. The established hierarchy of control methods enables more informed decisions about which intervention to apply based on robustness requirements, and raises questions about how personality interacts with other safety properties and whether personality control trade-offs exist with capabilities or alignment.
